subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Dark Matter: The Invisible Glue
description: A quick, practical guide to Dark Matter: The Invisible Glue.

hook.text: 85% of all matter in the universe is invisible. It doesn't emit or absorb light. We call it dark matter.

buildup.text: Fritz Zwicky noticed in 1933 that galaxies in clusters moved too fast. Visible mass couldn't explain their speed.

discovery.text: Vera Rubin confirmed it in the 1970s. Galaxy edges rotate as fast as their centers—impossible without extra hidden mass.

twist.text: We don't know what dark matter is. It's not black holes, not gas, not stars. It may be an undiscovered particle.

climax.text: Underground detectors and particle colliders are hunting for dark matter particles. So far, nothing has been found.

punchline.text: Eighty-five percent of all matter. Totally invisible. Still unknown.

quiz.question: How did scientists first detect evidence for dark matter?
options: Galaxies and clusters moved faster than visible matter alone could explain
Dark matter particles were captured in underground detectors
Telescopes observed dark regions blocking background starlight
